task: We’re supposed to get a cold snap later this week, and I’m trying to think ahead so I’m not scrambling if the power goes out. Could you help me put together a simple backup plan for staying warm and keeping the house in decent shape until things settle down?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it simple and focus on warmth, water, and safety.

## Backup plan for a cold snap

### 1) Make sure the main heat works now
- Turn the thermostat up and confirm the furnace starts normally
- Replace a dirty filter
- Make sure vents and returns are open
- Check for any odd noises, smells, or repeated shutoffs

### 2) Set up a safe backup heat option
- Have one room you can stay in if the power goes out
- Use a space heater only if it’s in good shape
- Keep it away from curtains, bedding, and clutter
- Plug it directly into the wall
- Treat it as **one-room, temporary heat only**

### 3) Protect the house
- Know where the main water shutoff is
- Insulate or cover exposed pipes if needed
- Disconnect outdoor hoses
- Keep cabinet doors open under sinks on cold outside walls
- Keep flashlights and batteries ready

### 4) Keep essentials handy
- blankets
- water
- food you can use without cooking much
- phone charger / power bank
- medications
- important numbers
- paper note with shutoffs and contacts

### 5) Decide your cutoff point early
Stay put if:
- roads glaze over
- power is flickering and you don’t need to go out
- wind is bringing down limbs or lines
- freezing rain starts

## Simple rule
If the furnace is working, the pipes are protected, and you have one safe backup room, you’re in decent shape for a short cold snap.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist** you can keep by the kitchen phone.

user_simulator.text: Pretty good. Plain enough I can use it. I’d like it a little tighter, though — more like an actual checklist I can print and keep by the phone. Include the water shutoff, the backup heater, and what to do first if the power fails at night. Also, I’d want a note about the pipes under the kitchen and bath sinks — that’s the sort of thing people forget.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter phone-side checklist.

## Cold snap backup checklist

### Before the storm
- [ ] Test the furnace / main heat
- [ ] Replace a dirty filter
- [ ] Keep vents and returns open
- [ ] Know where the main water shutoff is
- [ ] Open cabinet doors under kitchen and bath sinks if pipes are on an outside wall
- [ ] Set up one safe backup room
- [ ] Check backup heater
- [ ] Put out flashlights, batteries, blankets, water, and phone charger

### Backup heater
- [ ] Use only one room
- [ ] Use only as temporary heat
- [ ] Keep it away from curtains, bedding, and clutter
- [ ] Plug it directly into the wall

### If power fails at night
- [ ] Use flashlight, not candles if possible
- [ ] Check whether the outage is just your house or the neighborhood
- [ ] Keep the main water shutoff in mind
- [ ] Stay in the backup room if needed
- [ ] Keep pipes under kitchen and bath sinks open to warmer air if safe

### Stop and call for help if
- [ ] gas smell
- [ ] burning smell
- [ ] repeated breaker trips
- [ ] furnace won’t start
- [ ] pipes freeze or leak

If you want, I can make this into an even shorter **fridge-note version** with just the checkboxes.

user_simulator.text: That’ll do. Short enough to print and not fuss with. The sink pipes note is in there, water shutoff too, and the night steps are plain. I can use this.
